body:
This PR changes the default behaviour of picking up checkpoints. Instead of using the `∆t` obtained from the checkpoint, the model now defaults to the user-defined variables in the experiment file. 

Previously, if the user changed the `∆t`, it would be overwritten by the checkpointer saved state on pickup. This would make workflows like when, for example, spin up has a smaller dt and then dt is bumped up, impossible.

Now, the user-specified `∆t` will ALWAYS be used.

I have modified the test file to test for this, and made minimal changes to account for this. 

This is a breaking change; I will bump the version number when it is ready to merge.

labels.description: Concerning a change which breaks the API
labels.name: breaking change :broken_heart:

title: (v0.108.0) Always use user-defined `∆t` on `pickup`

body:
## Summary

- `Relaxation(rate=…, target=fts)` now accepts a `FieldTimeSeries` as the relaxation target.
- At materialize time, the FTS is wrapped in an internal `FieldTimeSeriesTarget{L,F}` carrying the simulation-side location (`instantiated_location(field)`) and the integer index of the forced field in `model_fields`. The `Relaxation` struct itself is unchanged (still `Relaxation{R,M,T}`); the target field's type just becomes `FieldTimeSeriesTarget` after materialization.
- A new kernel callable on `Relaxation{R,M,<:FieldTimeSeriesTarget}` reads `ϕ = model_fields[index][i,j,k]` and obtains `ϕᵣ` via `interpolate(X, Time(clock.time), fts, instantiated_location(fts), fts.grid)` (spatial + temporal interpolation), so the FTS may live on a different grid as long as its extent brackets the simulation grid in `x`, `y`, and `z`.
- Mismatched extents throw `ArgumentError` at materialize time naming the offending axis and both intervals.

The existing `Relaxation(target=callable)` / `Relaxation(target=number)` path is untouched — multiple dispatch routes those cases through the original `ContinuousForcing` wrapping.

## Why

This is the first slice of a small series that generalizes `Relaxation` for real-data use cases — driving an LES or regional run with prescribed large-scale state from a host model (ERA5, GFS, a parent simulation) through grid nudging and/or open boundaries with a fringe region. Scope of this PR is intentionally narrow: 3D mode only, FTS targets only. "Profile mode" (`mode=:profile` + column reference, see https://github.com/NumericalEarth/Breeze.jl/pull/562) and other target types are deferred to follow-up PRs.

## Design notes

- Mirrors the two existing patterns it composes: `Forcing(fts::FlavorOfFTS)` materializes into a discrete forcing (`forcing.jl:184-186`); `Relaxation` already mutates the user's recipe into a kernel-ready form at `materialize_forcing` (`relaxation.jl:75-78`). This PR keeps the `Relaxation` identity through materialization (`model.forcing.c isa Relaxation` stays `true`), only swapping the `target` field's type.
- Field index stored as runtime `Int`, matching the precedent in `ContinuousForcing.field_dependencies_indices` (`user_function_arguments.jl:1-19`) rather than encoding as a `Val{N}` type param.
- Existing `Forcing(fts)` doesn't enforce location matching (flagged latent constraint); this PR sidesteps that by using `interpolate`, so location/grid alignment between FTS and forced field is no longer required.

## Test plan

- [x] CPU unit tests pass locally (6/6: materialization wiring + analytical convergence + extent validation)
- [x] Existing `Relaxation` smoke tests (`GaussianMask`, `PiecewiseLinearMask`) still pass
- [x] Doctest output verified to match
- [ ] CI green (CPU + GPU)
- [ ] Reactant trace smoke test (deferred to follow-up unless CI catches a regression)

## Out of scope (follow-ups)

- Interpolation performance optimization by caching `FractionalIndices` arrays in `FieldTimeSeriesTarget` at materialize time.
- Profile mode (`mode=:profile`, column-averaged relaxation toward 1×1×Nz reference). Requires the new `update_forcing!` hook in each model's `update_state!`.
- Plain `Field` (time-invariant) targets.
- `column_field_time_series` utility for extracting a column reference from a 3D reanalysis FTS.
title: Support `FieldTimeSeries` targets in `Relaxation`

body: Closes #5599 This PR adds basic support for plotting scalar and one-dimensional time series recorded in a `FieldTimeSeries` object with Makie to complement existing support for plotting `Field` objects. The envisaged use case is to allow easy plotting of scalar diagnostics such as MOC strength at a particular latitude or one-dimensional diagnostics such as fields spatially averaged across horizontal dimensions recorded during a simulation using output writers and subsequently read in to a `FieldTimeSeries` from file. This can be achieved for scalar fields currently by indexing in to the field time series with a colon and explicitly passing the times as coordinates as in [stratified Kelvin-Helmholtz instability example](https://clima.github.io/OceananigansDocumentation/stable/literated/kelvin_helmholtz_instability#Pretty-things), though this will not work if the field time series is not loaded with the `InMemory()` backend (which for scalar time series is unlikely to be a problem admittedly). For one-dimensional field time series plotting requires quite a lot of manual work to account for indexing, getting relevant coordinate arrays etc. Some notes on design: - I have assumed that time should always be plotted on horizontal axis by default. - The conversion of the `FieldTimeSeries` data to a plottable array explicitly iterates over all time indices and stacks the arrays corresponding to the fields for each time index computed using the existing `make_plottable_array` method. For `FieldTimeSeries` with an `InMemory()` backend this is a bit inefficient but it keeps things working for case where `OnDisk()` backend is used and also allows reusing `make_plottable_array` method to deal with making / dropping singleton dimensions / transferring from GPU to CPU if necessary. As a simple example of how this can be used: ```julia using Oceananigans using Oceananigans.Units using CairoMakie grid = RectilinearGrid(size = (1, 10, 1), extent = (1, 1kilometers, 1)) output_times = 0:0.5hours:24hours field_time_series = FieldTimeSeries{Center, Center, Center}(grid, output_times) for (i, t) in enumerate(output_times) field = CenterField(grid) set!(field, (x, y, z) -> y * sinpi(t / 4hours)) set!(field_time_series, field, i) end contourf(field_time_series) ``` produces the following as output For more realistic examples, applying this to recorded outputs of MOC strength and horizontally spatially averaged temperature fields from some simulations I have been working on gives outputs like the following
title: Add support for plotting `FieldTimeSeries` in Makie extension
